video_title: The AI Mid-Cycle Slowdown: OpenAI, Anthropic, and the AI Price War
gist: Jordi Visser argues AI has entered a mid-cycle slowdown (not a bubble unwind): capital is rotating out of hyperscalers and the Mag 7 into a broad ~100-name thematic basket (small caps, chemicals/materials, power, healthcare, crypto), while a model-layer price war (OpenAI/Anthropic) collides with physical-layer inflation (PPI, indium phosphide, NAND bottlenecks). He stays AI-bullish as a secular trend but bearish on hyperscalers and model makers relative to the 'receivers' of the capex.

insights.claim: Visser is bearish on hyperscalers relative to the 'receivers' of AI capex and to beneficiaries like Eli Lilly, arguing the hyperscalers are financing the AI buildout, are in a race to commoditization, and may not ultimately get the money they expect.
insights.speaker: Jordi Visser
insights.so_what: Implies a pair trade: short/underweight hyperscalers, long the downstream beneficiaries and capex receivers.
insights.quote: I am bearish on the hyperscalers relative to the receivers of the money and relative to health care companies which will benefit like Eli Liy

insights.claim: Hyperscalers have posted back-to-back 4% down weeks relative to the S&P 500, an event seen only four times before in the prior three years, which Visser reads as a structural rotation rather than noise.
insights.speaker: Jordi Visser
insights.so_what: Confirms the rotation thesis with relative-strength data; watch hyperscaler/SPX relative ratio for continued breakdown.
insights.quote: We've had back-to-back 4% down weeks. We haven't seen that at any point. In fact, we've only had four before these two.

insights.claim: Small caps (IWM) are up 9.8% over the Mag 7 on a monthly return basis, an outperformance magnitude seen only twice in the past decade, signaling a large capital rotation underway.
insights.speaker: Jordi Visser
insights.so_what: Rare-event small-cap leadership argues against a bear market and supports rotating into breadth.
insights.quote: we're up 9.8% in IWM over the Mag 7 over the Mag 7. Uh this is back a decade. There's only two other times that this occurred.

insights.claim: Visser's thematic portfolio of 100 names carries ~$20 trillion total market cap versus the Mag 7's ~$22 trillion, framing the rotation as moving capital from 7 names into ~100 names with an average market cap near $150 billion.
insights.speaker: Jordi Visser
insights.so_what: A dispersion setup for options traders: lower correlation, higher single-name vol as money spreads across many mid-caps.
insights.quote: total market cap is 20 trillion. The market cap of the mag 7 is 22 trillion. This is the rotation.

insights.claim: Visser argues you cannot have a bear market while small caps make new all-time highs alongside rising energy prices and the market pricing in Fed rate hikes.
insights.speaker: Jordi Visser
insights.so_what: Breadth signal to stay constructive on equities broadly even as headline indices consolidate.
insights.quote: It's hard to have a bare market when small caps are at new all-time highs while we've got energy prices going higher.

insights.claim: Eight of the 11 S&P sectors are up month-to-date even as the index is down 2%, with leadership concentrated in non-consensus areas like industrials rather than the Mag 7 and semis, which are pulling back.
insights.speaker: Jordi Visser
insights.so_what: Confirms a rotation-driven tape; index weakness masks broadening participation favoring laggard sectors.
insights.quote: the S&P is down 2% monthto date and eight of the 11 sectors are now up for the month

insights.claim: Visser frames AI as being in a 'mid-cycle slowdown' where the second derivative of growth turns down once investors and analysts are fully positioned for acceleration, producing choppier returns and a lower Sharpe ratio rather than a bubble unwind or bear market.
insights.speaker: Jordi Visser
insights.so_what: Sets expectation for range-bound, rotation-driven markets; favor stock-picking and theme rotation over beta.
insights.quote: I believe we are in what is effectively a midcycle slowdown for AI.

insights.claim: Visser predicts retail-overinvested pockets (citing silver early in the year, gold for most of last year, Palantir) resolve through correction and consolidation inside a broader bull market, and expects the memory names to unwind the same way.
insights.speaker: Jordi Visser
insights.so_what: Treat sharp drawdowns in crowded AI/memory names as consolidations to buy, not regime change.
insights.quote: every bubble we have seen over the last few years. I don't care what it was. Silver at uh at the beginning of the year, gold for most of last year, Palunteer, these things unwind through a correction and then through consolidation with inside a bull market

insights.claim: Visser distinguishes being bearish on AI capex/revenue timing from being bearish on the stock market, arguing a capex or revenue delay does not imply a market collapse.
insights.speaker: Jordi Visser
insights.so_what: Lets investors stay long the broad market while hedging or avoiding the specific capex spenders.
insights.quote: you can be bearish on the capex and on the revenue delay, but that doesn't mean that you should be bearish on the stock market or a collapse. There are two totally different things

insights.claim: The token usage index is in a defined downtrend (biggest single-day drop on the most recent Friday), driven by rising open-source/Chinese model adoption plus seasonal summer usage declines, making a parabolic AI trade unlikely through summer.
insights.speaker: Jordi Visser
insights.so_what: Falling tokens pressure hyperscaler/model-maker revenue narratives; a tradeable bearish signal for the AI complex this summer.
insights.quote: this token thing is accelerating to the downside. It's a combination of more open- source usage, especially on the Chinese model side

insights.claim: Visser advises judging the source of bubble warnings, noting perma-bear Peter Berezin started calling an AI bubble in mid-2025 after first calling for a recession in mid-2024, so his bearishness reflects standing bias rather than new signal.
insights.speaker: Jordi Visser
insights.so_what: Filter perma-bear commentary by track record before acting on viral bearish posts.
insights.quote: he started calling for a recession in mid 2024. He was bullish relative to consensus there. The reason I bring this up is these people will never change.

themes.theme: AI price war and the 'money loop'

insights.claim: Sam Altman has admitted OpenAI is considering drastic price cuts in anticipation of a war for users, and that AI costs became a major customer complaint in early 2026 after no one cared about cost before.
insights.speaker: Jordi Visser
insights.so_what: A model-layer price war compresses margins for model makers and the hyperscalers selling them compute; a key bear catalyst to watch.
insights.quote: OpenAI considers drastic price cuts, anticipating war for users.

insights.claim: Visser argues Anthropic's revenue will not scale anywhere near its projections because the first five months of 2026 were a one-time scramble of enterprises switching from ChatGPT Enterprise, and any pause in Anthropic's parabolic ARR would be a big market negative.
insights.speaker: Jordi Visser
insights.so_what: Watch Anthropic ARR growth deceleration as a sentiment trigger across the AI complex; reason to avoid model makers.
insights.quote: I don't know who hasn't figured out yet, but Enthropic's revenue is not going to scale anywhere close to what they've been projecting.

insights.claim: Sophisticated customers including Microsoft, Meta and Uber are reportedly cancelling or cutting back AI spending because costs are too high, signaling demand-side pushback on AI pricing.
insights.speaker: Jordi Visser
insights.so_what: Cancellations by the most sophisticated buyers undercut the insatiable-demand narrative; monitor enterprise AI spend guidance.
insights.quote: when you're seeing Microsoft talk about cancelling things, when you're talking about Meta, when you got Uber, you've got the most sophisticated places on the planet

insights.claim: Visser frames the central AI tension as price compression at the model layer versus inflation at the physical layer, predicting the next margin squeeze moves toward hyperscalers, cloud providers and lease-capacity players.
insights.speaker: Jordi Visser
insights.so_what: Identifies where margins get squeezed next; positions away from compute sellers and toward physical-layer suppliers.
insights.quote: The key tension in AI is now price compression at the model layer versus inflation at the physical layer.

insights.claim: Visser cites Morgan Stanley's characterization of the circular AI financing relationship as the 'most dangerous money loop in history' and warns that any disruption inside the loop is a negative for the complex.
insights.speaker: Jordi Visser
insights.so_what: Circular vendor financing is the structural fragility; watch for free-cash-flow burn and credit signals to break the loop.
insights.quote: this circular relationship or mapped as Morgan Stanley said, as the most dangerous money loop in history

insights.claim: Coinbase CEO Brian Armstrong predicts 80% of AI workloads will run on 99%-cheaper models within 12-18 months, a view Visser fully endorses and links to a coming good-enough open-source model that runs locally on a ~$4,000 laptop.
insights.speaker: Jordi Visser (citing Brian Armstrong)
insights.so_what: Edge/local inference commoditizes the model layer and undercuts cloud AI revenue; bullish for edge hardware, bearish for compute rentiers.
insights.quote: 80% of the workloads will be running on 99% cheaper models within 12 to 18 months.

insights.claim: Investment-grade credit (CDX IG) and the BDC/junk indices are still consolidating near highs with no stress despite more private-credit gates and bad news, which Visser says rules out a genuine bubble unwind because a real unwind would show up first in credit.
insights.speaker: Jordi Visser
insights.so_what: Use credit spreads as the tell; their calm is the strongest evidence against an imminent crash.
insights.quote: Right now, we're not seeing anything on the credit side. Always remember that if we're really having a bubble unwind, the credit place will take part of it.

insights.claim: Core CPI is running 4.2% year-over-year but Visser thinks it likely peaked given gas prices fell from $4.55 to $4.08, while PCE still tracks ~0.4% and PPI posted its biggest jump on record, keeping the Fed stuck on sticky inflation.
insights.speaker: Jordi Visser
insights.so_what: Sticky inflation keeps the Fed on hold and rate hikes on the table; constrains multiple expansion and favors real-asset trades.
insights.quote: CPI is up 4.2 year. While it may be sticky, which I agree completely with this, it likely just peaked.

insights.claim: Visser reports rate hikes are back on the table with a new Fed chair incoming, and he is hearing growing expectations that the Fed leans hawkish at the next meeting.
insights.speaker: Jordi Visser
insights.so_what: Hawkish Fed risk plus a leadership transition raises policy uncertainty; weigh against rate-sensitive positioning.
insights.quote: rate hikes are back on the table again. We don't know what's going to go on, but we just know that new Fed chair is going and it creates a lot of uncertainty.

insights.claim: Citing Jeff deGraaf, Visser notes a record 50-year beta and momentum purge coincided with equal-weight breadth expanding back to 63% of names above their 20-day moving average, which he reads as a bullish factor unwind/rotation rather than a market top.
insights.speaker: Jordi Visser (citing Jeff deGraaf)
insights.so_what: A violent factor unwind that broadens breadth supports staying long the broad market through the rotation.
insights.quote: the equal weight breath expanded back to 63% of the names above their 20-day moving average. That's just a bullish sign.

insights.claim: Visser calls chemicals 'the oil of the next 10 years' as the AI industrial economy shifts from barrels burned to molecules engineered, noting the chemicals index just broke out after ~5 years of consolidation and now sits as the third-best theme in his portfolio.
insights.speaker: Jordi Visser
insights.so_what: Positions chemicals/materials as a core AI-physical-layer beneficiary; a fresh technical breakout to accumulate.
insights.quote: I think chemicals are the oil of the next 10 years.

insights.claim: Visser spotlights Entegris (ENTG) as a lagging chemical name near all-time highs that just ripped, with NAND mentioned 39 times across its last two earnings calls and framed as a 2026 transition tailwind tied to the Vera Rubin rollout's rising complexity.
insights.speaker: Jordi Visser
insights.so_what: A specific cheap-laggard long: materials/bonding supplier leveraged to NAND and next-gen AI infrastructure complexity.
insights.quote: How often did they say the word NAND on their call? 39 times in the last two quarters.

insights.claim: An LLM ranking of Visser's themes (run via Claude Co-work) put 'the whole rack' first and chemicals/materials second, with top chemical names ENTG, MKSI, MU and TSM.
insights.speaker: Jordi Visser
insights.so_what: Concrete watchlist of materials/semis names to screen as physical-layer rotation beneficiaries.
insights.quote: for chemicals, it says ENTG, MKSI, MU, TSM.

insights.claim: Indium phosphide is highlighted as a China-dominated supply-chain bottleneck constraining AI data center construction, alongside high-end MLCC supply being squeezed by AI servers.
insights.speaker: Jordi Visser
insights.so_what: Supply bottlenecks pace the AI buildout and create pricing power for component suppliers; a geopolitical risk vector to monitor.
insights.quote: indium phosphate phosphide is one of the several supply chain bottlenecks collectively constraining AI data center construction.

insights.claim: Citing Dan Dryfus, Visser says humanity may need as much copper in the next 18 years as it mined over the last 10,000, forcing US government support for mining, and links collapsing silver prices to future demand from data centers and solid-state batteries.
insights.speaker: Jordi Visser (citing Dan Dryfus)
insights.so_what: Structural commodity demand thesis: accumulate copper/silver and miners as AI/electrification scarcity trades.
insights.quote: we might need as much copper in the next 18 years as humanity mined over the last 10,000.

insights.claim: Visser argues the orbital/space data center build is a catalyst for a silver boom, tying it to the SpaceX IPO narrative and the far larger silver requirements of data centers in space versus terrestrial.
insights.speaker: Jordi Visser
insights.so_what: Speculative second-order silver demand driver; watch SpaceX IPO progress as a sentiment catalyst for silver.
insights.quote: Here's why the Space X IPO could be the start of a silver boom.

themes.theme: Data center economics and government risk

insights.claim: xAI reportedly spent ~$40 billion building Colossus and Colossus 2 and, based on Anthropic/Google licensing disclosures, will be paid ~$26 billion per year to license the compute, implying an ~18-month payback that Visser says argues against a data-center bubble.
insights.speaker: Jordi Visser
insights.so_what: An 18-month payback on the physical asset supports the build economics even if model-maker revenue is fragile.
insights.quote: he will get paid 26 billion per year to license the compute from these data centers. That is a payback period of 18 months.

insights.claim: China is preparing a ~$300 billion AI buildout plan, having decided AGI is close, which Visser reads as a geopolitical step-change signaling they 'smell blood' as models rapidly improve.
insights.speaker: Jordi Visser
insights.so_what: State-scale Chinese AI spend intensifies the compute race and critical-minerals competition; bullish for the buildout supply chain.
insights.quote: they've decided that AGI is close. So they are accelerating and prepping a $300 billion plan to fund the AI buildout.

insights.claim: Visser sees rising odds (a referenced 34% probability) of governments taking stakes in AI companies and views eventual government control as a negative for hyperscalers and model makers as AI becomes critical to biology and national security.
insights.speaker: Jordi Visser
insights.so_what: Government intervention/equity-stake risk is a structural overhang on AI equity multiples; a tail risk to price.
insights.quote: I think this would be a big negative if the government is taking stakes in these companies. 34% chance.

insights.claim: Visser argues AI-driven drug discovery makes healthcare and Eli Lilly an attractive entry given the sector's deep underperformance and recent shift to outperformance, spotlighting Chai Discovery (backed by Pfizer and Eli Lilly) as a key startup.
insights.speaker: Jordi Visser
insights.so_what: Underperforming healthcare is a contrarian AI-beneficiary rotation; ENT/biotech leverage to AI protein design.
insights.quote: I just think healthcare is a great place to be looking at this point, guys.

insights.claim: Visser is converting his YouTube output to a crypto-forward format in September, providing thematic portfolios of both tokens and public equities, on the thesis that crypto is a major part of the application layer of AI benefiting from agentic AI and crypto rails.
insights.speaker: Jordi Visser
insights.so_what: Flags crypto/tokenized equities as a forward scarcity-software bull theme to position ahead of institutional adoption.
insights.quote: I believe that crypto will be a major story.

insights.claim: Visser states Bitcoin is in a bear market because it sits below its 200-day moving average and only hanging on the 200-week, and he will not call it bullish until it reclaims the 200-day, yet he continues to accumulate small amounts.
insights.speaker: Jordi Visser
insights.so_what: Technical line in the sand: the 200-day reclaim is the trigger to shift from accumulation to bullish on Bitcoin.
insights.quote: We are below the 200 day. We are in a bare market in Bitcoin. There's no way to look at this and think we're not until we get above the 200 day
